claims.claim: J-Vers used Next.js for frontend performance/SEO, with R3F, Three.js, GSAP, and Tailwind as key frontend libraries.
Sparkk's Awwwards case study lists Nuxt, Three.js, Lenis, GSAP, Figma, Blender, and Strapi for an interactive 3D site.
Codrops' 3D product grid stack is Next.js, React Three Fiber, Tailwind, Motion, and GLSL.
Radix Primitives are positioned as accessible, unstyled primitives for high-quality design systems and web apps, with focus and keyboard behavior handled.
shadcn/ui exposes common components including button, card, dialog, drawer/sheet, empty, input, navigation, popover, sheet, skeleton, sonner, tabs, toast, tooltip, and typography.
shadcn's React Hook Form guide combines React Hook Form, Zod, accessible field markup, errors, and Sonner feedback.
Motion for React covers production UI animation, gestures, layout animation, scroll-triggered/linked animation, SVG animation, and reduced-motion hooks.
gsap.matchMedia scopes and reverts animations/ScrollTriggers and can branch on prefers-reduced-motion.
GSAP and previously paid Club plugins became free for all users and commercial use in 2025.
React Three Fiber is a React renderer for Three.js with ecosystem helpers including drei, gltfjsx, and postprocessing.
Lenis is a smooth scroll library for modern browsers and documents GSAP ScrollTrigger integration.
21st combines AI-powered component generation with a React/Tailwind community library of components, screens, and themes for coding assistants.
React Bits positions animated React components as AI-ready, copyable into projects, with JS/TS and CSS/Tailwind variants.
Magic UI describes 150+ animated components built with React, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, and Motion, as a companion for shadcn/ui.
Aceternity's catalog repeatedly groups hero sections, bento grids, pricing, navbars, footers, empty states, text animations, scroll/parallax, magnetic buttons, animated modals, and tooltips.
Olivier Larose frames repeated award-site animations around scroll progress, viewport detection, sticky positioning, easing, text splitting, interpolation, and shaders.
Olivier's Awwwards gallery tutorial uses Next.js/GSAP-style mouse interaction and treats the floating gallery as a recurring Awwwards interaction style.
Design engineering quality depends on implementation details, states, transition taste, and knowing when not to animate.
The Syntax/GSAP discussion emphasizes GSAP's strength across text, SVG, performance, React, and accessibility concerns.
Transcript term extraction shows repeated implementation focus on scroll, text, Next, React, bento, hero, GSAP, Tailwind, and cursor interactions.
The Prismic long-form tutorial heavily repeats Next, React, Tailwind, GSAP, Three.js, TypeScript, performance, hero, and scroll.
DesignCourse shows shadcn plus Cursor can produce a distinct landing page when prompts and design decisions avoid a generic shadcn look.
Codegrid's 3D scroll product tutorial centers scroll, Next, Motion, Three.js, GSAP, and performance for a product page interaction.
A Reddit frontend animation thread repeatedly recommends GSAP/ScrollTrigger, Framer Motion/Motion, Three.js/R3F, and warns about timing, mobile performance, and scroll orchestration.
React animation community advice separates CSS for simple effects, Motion for component/UI animation, and GSAP for complex timelines.
Motion-driven components on shadcn primitives focus on microinteractions, morphing layouts, dropdowns/dialogs, buttons, and scroll-driven transitions.
Community feedback on animated component libraries specifically calls for prefers-reduced-motion support and warns against too much bounce or sluggish timing.
Practitioners warn that Awwwards-style inspiration can be too experimental, slow, scroll-hijacking, or unusable for real client/product work.
R3F is praised for React integration and ecosystem, but raw Three.js can be preferable for low-level simulations and performance-sensitive rendering control.
A recent cinematic interactive 3D build publicly lists Three.js, R3F, GSAP, and GLSL as the stack.
Tim Gabe argues gamification works when it reflects user competence/progress, not decorative points, badges, or leaderboards.
Tim Gabe's app design formula emphasizes a system that derisks steps from concept to world-class app instead of relying on isolated taste.
